title: Access Control for Agentic AI
description:
## Least Privilege for AI Agents

A team is building an **agentic** application: a Bedrock-powered agent that calls internal tools and APIs **on behalf of individual users**. Security requires that each agent invocation acts with a **scoped identity** tied to the requesting user — not a single shared, broadly-permissioned credential — and that access to downstream resources is governed and auditable.

Which approach best meets this requirement?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Identity to give the agent a managed, scoped identity and govern its access to tools/resources per user
Create one IAM user with a long-lived access key that the agent uses for every request
Embed a static API key in the agent's prompt template so it can call the tools
Give the agent the same administrator role the developers use, for simplicity

explanation: Agentic workloads need identity and least-privilege access scoped to the acting user/session. Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Identity provides managed identity and access controls for agents, so each invocation is governed, scoped, and auditable. A single shared IAM user with a long-lived key (b), a static key in the prompt (c), or reusing an admin role (d) all violate least privilege, can't attribute actions to a user, and create a large blast radius if leaked.

hints.title: Per-user scope, not shared creds
hints.body: The phrase 'on behalf of individual users' with a scoped identity rules out any single shared or admin credential.
